person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
Mary (—?—) Wilcox: End-of-Line Ancestor of Unknown Origins

(1) BIRTH, PARENTS AND MAIDEN SURNAME OF MARY, WIFE OF JOHN WILCOX OR WILCOCKS, IS UNKNOWN OR CANNOT BE VERIFIED - no adequate evidence that it was Mary Wylsher, Wylshire or Farnsworth  (See Discussion item for details). VIEW BIOGRAPHICAL ITEMS IN STORY (MEMORIES) FOR THIS RECORD, LKSN-6VZ, and biographical information below ​  

(2)  See also BIOGRAPHICAL SKETCH FROM "HALE, HOUSE AND RELATED FAMILIES...," BY JACOBUS AND WATERMAN. pgs 803-805:
John Wilcox, born in England, died at Hartford, Conn., 1 Oct. 1651. He was an original settler of Hartford, and his lands were entered in February 1639/40. Besides his houselot of two acres, he had various parcels, one of four acres of upland, two acres beyond Rocky Hill, two acres and twenty acres of upland, three acres bought of Francis Andrews, forty-one acres bought of Richard Risley and others, bounded on land of John Hall, Sr., and George Hubbard (this last was sold to John Bidwell, April 1666), an acre bought of Ralph Keeler, over five acres obtained by exchange with John Cullick, over two acres bought of Thomas Bull, three acres bounded on the Great River, and five acres bought of James Coles. He was a "payle-maker" and seems to have been materially successful, leaving an estate of moderate size, and having two apprentices, a boy and a girl. He served on a jury, 5 June 1645. The inventory of his estate, taken by John Talcott, Andrew Bacon and John Barnard, amounted to 391.03.00 (pounds, sp) His will, dated 24 July 1651, calls him "payle maker," and made elaborate provision for his wife Mary, who doubtless had been his helpmate for many years and was mother of his children. He gave her 20 (pounds, sp) to be paid in corn (30 bushels in winter wheat, 10 bushels in summer wheat, 20 bushels in barley, 20 bushes in rye, 40 bushels in Indian corn), two cows; my best hogs at Mattabeseck (Middletown), two of my best shoats at home, my colt, and "my owld house" to dwell in for life, my son to keep it in repair. She was also to have a third of the fruit, and was to be supplied with wood. She was to have 4 (pounds) by the year; if she moves her dwelling or marries, the fruit and wood are not to be supplied, but she is to have 6 (pounds) per year. He also gave her "the bed I lie on" and specifed articles. To his servant Samuel, he gave two suits of apparel, etc., according to the indenture. To his daughter, Ann Hall, 20 (pounds); and 20 (pounds) at the death of my wife, to her or her children, but if she be died without issue, then 10 (pounds) to her husband, John Hall, if living, and the other 10 (pounds) to the children of John Biddell (Bidwell). To his daughter, Sarah Biddell, his mare and two steers, also a cart and a plow with a share and coulter. To John Biddell's children, 40 (pounds), to be equally divided among Sarah Biddell's surviving children. His wife at death was to give 25 (pounds) to Sarah Wilcock, daughter of son John. To son, John Willcock, the residue, he to be executor. His maid, Elizabeth Wakeman, was to have 20 shillings at the end of apprenticeship. Andrew Bacon and John Barnard to be overseers, and they, with George Grave, witnessed the will. On 27 Oct. 1667, the Court ordered John Wilcox [Jr.] to pay his mother 6 (pounds) a year, as on account of weakness she cannot occupy "the Old House." Probably she had moved sometime before that to her daughter Bidwell's home. She made her will, 4 Oct. 1666. She gave to her "Consin" (granddaughter), best fellow pillow, residue to son-in-law, John Bidwell, he to be executor. Friends, Beacon Butler and James Ensign, to be overseers. Children of John and Mary ( ) Wilcox, born in England:
i. Ann, b abt 1616 d at Middletown, Conn., 20 July 1673 ea. about 57; m: John Hall, b in England abt. 1619, d. at Middletown, 22 Jan 1694/5 ae, son of John Hall, Sr., of Hartford and Middletown. He m. (2) at Middletown 1 Oct. 1674, Mqry (Curtice) Hubbard, who d 29 June 1709, widow of Thomas Hubbard. His will, dated 23 May 1691, named relatives of his own and of his second wife; no children. ii. Sarah, b. abt. 1618; d. at Hartford, 15 June 1690; m: John Bidwell (Bidwell Family, No. 1), who d. at Hartford in 1687. iii. John, b (say 1620); d. 24 May 1676z

zh.lifeSummary: Mary於大約1594年出生在英國。她於大約1615年與John Wilcox Sr.在英國結婚。他們至少是1個兒子和2個女兒的父母。她去世於1668年12月在布利提申 可隆尼亞爾 亞美利加康涅狄格州哈特福德哈特福德享年75歲。

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

historicEvents.title: The Pilgrims Board the Mayflower
historicEvents.description: "The Pilgrims boarded the Mayflower in search of religious freedom in the New World in September 1620. The Mayflower set sail from Plymouth, England, with 102 passengers, 40 of whom called themselves ""Saints."" After two stormy months at sea, the ship reached the New World. Nearly half of the group died during their first winter due to malnutrition and the harsh New England winter."

en.lifeSummary: Mary was born about 1594, in England. She married John Wilcox Sr. about 1615, in England. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 2 daughters. She died in December 1668, in Hartford, Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America, at the age of 75.
